attrs.alt: Authentication Strength assurance pyramid — Phishing-Resistant at top, MFA at base, Custom at bottom

content.text: Authentication strength is a named policy that defines an allowlist of authentication method combinations. When a CA grant control references a strength, Entra ID checks whether the user's completed authentication satisfies at least one combination in the allowlist. If none match, the user is prompted to authenticate with an allowed method.

Authentication strength replaces the blunt 'Require MFA' built-in control. Where 'Require MFA' accepts any registered MFA method (including SMS OTP), a strength policy lets you restrict to specific methods — for example, phishing-resistant methods only for admin accounts.
All registered MFA methods including SMS, voice, TOTP, Authenticator push/OTP, FIDO2, WHfB, CBA, TAP (multi-use)
FIDO2 security key, Windows Hello for Business, Microsoft Authenticator (passwordless), CBA
No-password mandate; BYOD or managed devices with modern auth
FIDO2 security key, Windows Hello for Business, Certificate-based authentication only
Admin portals, privileged access, regulated data; defeats AiTM proxy attacks

Phishing-resistant methods (FIDO2, WHfB, hardware CBA) cannot be intercepted by adversary-in-the-middle proxy attacks because they cryptographically bind the authentication to the specific origin. SMS and Authenticator push are NOT phishing-resistant — an AiTM proxy can relay the push approval transparently.
FIDO2 hardware key (e.g., YubiKey); strongest available method
TPM-backed biometric/PIN bound to device; works on Entra-joined Windows 10/11
Smart card or hardware-based certificate; used in federal/DoD environments
Number matching reduces MFA fatigue; still vulnerable to AiTM
Authenticator passwordless phone sign-in; requires device registration
TOTP from authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Authy, etc.)
SMS OTP; weakest MFA factor — susceptible to SIM-swap; avoid if possible
Phone call OTP; similar risk to SMS; avoid for high-assurance scenarios
TAP multi-use: qualifies for MFA strength. TAP one-time: does not qualify

Built-in strength GUIDs end in a fixed suffix (000...0002 through 000...0004). They are globally consistent and never change — safe to hard-code in Terraform or scripts. Use the Graph API to inspect which combinations each built-in strength allows.

# List all authentication strength policies (built-in and custom)
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Policy.Read.All'

$strengths = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationStrengthPolicies' \
    -OutputType PSObject

foreach ($s in $strengths.value) {
    Write-Host "ID: $($s.id)"
    Write-Host "Name: $($s.displayName)"
    Write-Host "Type: $($s.policyType)"  # built_in or custom
    Write-Host "Combinations: $($s.allowedCombinations -join ', ')"
    Write-Host '---'
}

# Get a specific built-in strength by the fixed GUID
$phishResistant = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationStrengthPolicies/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000004' \
    -OutputType PSObject
Write-Host $phishResistant.allowedCombinations

Custom strengths let you define a precise allowlist. Use cases include: restricting to hardware-bound methods only (FIDO2 + WHfB), excluding SMS/voice from an otherwise standard MFA policy, or creating a regional strength for users in countries without reliable FIDO2 hardware availability.

# Create a custom authentication strength for an org that wants
# only FIDO2 and WHfB (no Authenticator push or OTP — stricter than Passwordless MFA)
$body = @{
    DisplayName = 'Hardware-only MFA'
    Description = 'Requires FIDO2 or WHfB; excludes all software-based methods'
    AllowedCombinations = @(
        'fido2',
        'windowsHelloForBusiness',
        'x509CertificateMultiFactor'
    )
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 3

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationStrengthPolicies' \
    -Body $body -ContentType 'application/json'

# Update a custom strength to add a new allowed combination
# (PATCH only updates AllowedCombinations — name/description can also be changed)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationStrengthPolicies/<custom-id>' \
    -Body (@{ AllowedCombinations = @('fido2', 'windowsHelloForBusiness', 'microsoftAuthenticatorPush') } | ConvertTo-Json) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'
A custom strength can include any subset of the full combination list — choose combinations your user population can actually satisfy.
Updating a custom strength's allowedCombinations immediately affects all CA policies that reference it — test with Report-Only first.
Custom strengths cannot be deleted if any CA policy references them — remove the reference first.
Built-in strengths (000...0002–0004) cannot be modified or deleted.
Using Authentication Strength in CA Policies

In a CA grant controls block, use 'authenticationStrength' with the strength's ID instead of 'mfa' in builtInControls. The two cannot be combined in the same grant block — choose one or the other. Authentication strength is the recommended approach for all new policies.
Migrating from 'Require MFA' to Authentication Strength

The 'mfa' built-in control is functionally equivalent to the 'Multifactor authentication' built-in strength (ID: ...000000000002). Migrating to authentication strength gives you an audit trail of exactly which methods satisfy each policy and enables future tightening (e.g., removing SMS) without creating a new policy.

Migration is low-risk because the Multifactor authentication strength covers the same methods as the 'mfa' built-in control. Start there, then gradually tighten to Passwordless or Phishing-resistant for admin accounts.

The azuread_authentication_strength_policy resource manages custom strengths. Reference it by ID in the CA policy's grant_controls block. This keeps strength definitions version-controlled and prevents ad-hoc portal edits.

The most common failure mode when deploying authentication strength is that users do not have a qualifying method registered. Deploy in Report-Only mode first, then use sign-in logs to identify affected users before enforcement.
User has only SMS registered; policy requires Passwordless MFA
Help user register FIDO2 key or Authenticator passwordless before enabling the policy
Guest user has no registered method in the resource tenant
Enable cross-tenant MFA trust in Inbound settings, or require guests to use TAP for initial registration
User tries to use Authenticator push but policy requires phishing-resistant
User must register FIDO2 or WHfB; provide hardware key issuance process
Policy uses custom strength with unsupported combination for legacy client
Enable Report-Only first; check which apps and clients would be affected before enforcing

Use SigninLogs in Log Analytics to understand what methods users are currently satisfying MFA with. This data drives the business case for method migration (e.g., moving from SMS to Authenticator) and identifies users who will be blocked when a stricter strength is enforced.

// KQL: breakdown of authentication methods used to satisfy CA auth strength
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where ConditionalAccessStatus == 'success'
| mv-expand AuthenticationDetails
| extend Method = tostring(AuthenticationDetails.authenticationMethod)
| extend Result = tostring(AuthenticationDetails.succeeded)
| where Result == 'true'
| summarize Count = count() by Method
| order by Count desc

// KQL: find users still using SMS or voice OTP despite MFA strength policy
// (useful for planning migration to stronger methods)
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(30d)
| where ConditionalAccessStatus == 'success'
| mv-expand AuthenticationDetails
| where AuthenticationDetails.authenticationMethod in ('SMS', 'Voice call')
| where tostring(AuthenticationDetails.succeeded) == 'true'
| summarize LastSeen = max(TimeGenerated), Count = count() by UserPrincipalName
| order by Count desc
Authentication strength defines an allowlist of method combinations; CA evaluates the user's completed auth against this list.
Three built-in strengths cover the most common use cases: MFA (broadest), Passwordless MFA, and Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WHfB/CBA only).
Custom strengths let you define any subset of combinations — useful for hardware-only mandates or excluding weak methods like SMS.
Authentication strength replaces the 'mfa' built-in control and is the recommended approach for all new CA policies.
Deploy new strengths in Report-Only mode first; use SigninLogs to identify users without qualifying methods before enforcing.
Phishing-resistant methods (FIDO2, WHfB, hardware CBA) are the only methods immune to AiTM proxy attacks — mandate them for all privileged accounts.
